medications.indication_rationale: Rapid reduction of aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt) by controlling both heart rate and blood pressure is the definitive first-line medical management of dissection, halting flap propagation. This is delivered intravenously by intensive-care paramedics and in hospital — the AI Doctor CANNOT administer it and instead recognises the need and escalates via 000. Beta-blockade must precede any vasodilator to avoid reflex tachycardia.

Intravenous analgesia relieves severe pain and reduces the sympathetic surge that drives blood pressure and aortic shear. In the source note the attending clinician gave morphine 5 mg IV; in telehealth the AI Doctor cannot administer IV drugs — this is delivered by paramedics/ED. Recorded as part of the correct emergency pathway, not an AI-Doctor action.
medications.dose_route_frequency: morphine 5 mg IV (administered by intensive-care paramedics / emergency department, not by the AI Doctor)

medications.indication_rationale: Antithrombotic therapy given on a mistaken assumption of myocardial infarction is dangerous in aortic dissection — it markedly increases the risk of catastrophic haemorrhage and rupture. This is a critical error of commission to avoid until dissection is excluded by imaging.

medications.drug_name: pure vasodilator without prior rate control (e.g. hydralazine or immediate-release nifedipine)
medications.indication_rationale: Lowering blood pressure with a vasodilator before beta-blockade causes reflex tachycardia, raising dP/dt and aortic wall shear, which can worsen dissection propagation. Rate control must come first; this is an in-hospital titration decision, never an AI-Doctor recommendation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once stabilised, take antihypertensive medication (perindopril and indapamide) every day exactly as prescribed and never let the prescription lapse; arrange automatic repeats and regular BP review.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The dissection occurred against a background of severe hypertension made worse by stopping the tablets weeks ago; sustained blood-pressure control is the single most important measure to prevent recurrence and propagation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Complete smoking cessation with support (Quitline, nicotine replacement or pharmacotherapy via GP).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Smoking accelerates arterial wall damage and aneurysm/dissection risk; stopping reduces the shear-related stress on vessel walls.

patient_education_points.point: This is a medical emergency — you must not get up or move around, because movement and a rising blood pressure can push the tear further along the vessel.
The paramedics will start a strong intravenous drip to bring your blood pressure and heart rate down quickly and stop the tearing, and you will need an urgent scan at hospital.
Try to relax your muscles and take slow, steady breaths — staying calm helps keep your blood pressure down while help is on the way.
Taking your blood-pressure tablets every day and stopping smoking removes the main forces that stress and tear the blood-vessel walls, preventing this from happening again.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate emergency transfer now (000 ambulance to a tertiary centre); vascular surgery / ICU follow-up and lifelong BP management thereafter
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: airway/breathing/circulation and conscious level en route; in hospital: dissection extent and malperfusion on CT angiography, blood pressure and heart-rate control, limb and organ perfusion
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any drop in consciousness, worsening pain, or signs of collapse — update 000 immediately for possible rupture/haemorrhagic shock

must_include_items: Recognise a life-threatening vascular emergency from abrupt tearing migrating back pain plus an acutely ischaemic (pale, cold, weak) leg
Call 000 for an emergency ambulance without delay
Instruct the patient to lie flat and stay completely still, avoiding any exertion
Do NOT give aspirin or any anticoagulant, and do not treat as musculoskeletal back pain
Convey that definitive care is urgent hospital imaging (CT angiography) and intravenous blood-pressure/heart-rate control
acceptable_alternatives: Advising the patient to keep the front door unlocked / arrange access for paramedics, given they are alone
Staying on the line with the patient until the ambulance arrives
errors_of_omission: Failing to recognise the emergency and triaging as back pain / renal colic
Failing to advise the patient to stay still and flat
errors_of_commission: Recommending aspirin, an antiplatelet, an anticoagulant or thrombolysis (treating as MI)
Advising simple analgesia and rest at home, or self-transport / driving to hospital
Attempting to manage or reassure at home rather than escalating
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES immediate 000 escalation AND keeping the patient still/flat. Triaging as benign back pain, or recommending an antithrombotic, is a critical automatic failure regardless of other content.
